attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old woman is being reviewed on the ward one day after surgical repair of a fractured neck of femur. For her pain management, she was ordered 5–10 mg oral oxycodone hourly as required and has been receiving doses of 10 mg oxycodone. She was also prescribed regular oral paracetamol 1 g every six hours. The nursing team have asked for the patient to be reviewed as she appeared to be “drowsier than normal” with a sedation score of two, and previously assessed as having moderate-to-severe intensity pain with minimal functional limitation. Her medical history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease diagnosed five years ago.

On review, the patient is sleeping on the bed. Her respiratory rate is 13 breaths/min. She looks drowsy but is easily roused. She answers all the questions appropriately, however she is unable to stay awake during the consultation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Withhold oxycodone until the patient is more awake
No action is needed as patient's respiratory rate is not low
Cease oral oxycodone and administer buprenorphine transdermal patch
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of excessive opioid administration. Opioid is a high-risk drug that is associated with several serious side effects including opioid-induced ventilatory impairment (OIVI). There are several risk factors for OIVI, although patients without any risk factors may develop OIVI as well. Patient factors include: advanced age, obesity, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and renal disease. Medication factors include: continuous infusions, use of more than one opioid analgesic or formulation, modified-release opioid analgesics, and co-prescribed sedatives. OIVI is also more likely to occur within the first 24 hours after surgery.

Patients who are receiving opioid should be closely monitored with regular documentation of their sedation scores. The general principle is that clinicians should aim for a sedation score of less than two. It is important to note that respiratory rate is not a reliable indicator of developing OIVI as patients with severe OIVI may have a normal respiratory rate. Increasing sedation is a more reliable clinical indicator of OIVI.

Withholding oxycodone until the patient is more awake is the most appropriate management in this scenario. The patient is at risk of opioid-induced ventilatory impairment, and increasing sedation is an early warning sign. Withholding further opioid, reducing subsequent doses, and increasing monitoring are essential steps to prevent progression to respiratory depression.

Initiating a medical emergency call is not indicated as the patient is easily roused and able to answer questions appropriately. This would be appropriate if the patient was difficult to rouse or unconscious (sedation score of three).

Administering intravenous naloxone is reserved for patients who are difficult to rouse or unconscious, or who have significant respiratory depression. In this case, naloxone is not required as the patient is easily roused and maintaining an adequate airway and respiratory rate.

No action is needed as patient's respiratory rate is not low is incorrect. Respiratory rate alone is not a reliable indicator of opioid-induced ventilatory impairment, and increasing sedation should prompt intervention even if the respiratory rate is within normal limits.

Ceasing oral oxycodone and administering buprenorphine transdermal patch is not recommended for post-operative pain management because the slow onset and offset of buprenorphine patches make rapid titration impossible and do not address the immediate risk of opioid toxicity.

Patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) is an important strategy in post-operative pain management. PCA allows the patient to self-administer small, controlled doses of opioid, which reduces the risk of overdose by preventing excessive dosing by staff or overestimation of pain needs. PCA devices are programmed with lockout intervals to prevent repeated dosing within a short period, further reducing the risk of OIVI. In patients at risk of opioid toxicity, PCA can provide effective pain relief while minimising the risk of oversedation and respiratory depression. Consideration of PCA may be appropriate in this patient once she is more alert and her sedation score is less than two.
learningPoints: Increasing sedation is a more reliable indicator of opioid-induced ventilatory impairment (OIVI) than respiratory rate.
If a patient has a sedation score of 2, withhold opioid until the patient is more awake and reduce subsequent opioid doses regardless of pain scores.
references: Schug, A. Palmer, G, et. al. Acute Pain Management: Scientific Evidence (Fifth Edition). Australian and New Zealand College of Anaesthetists and Faculty of Pain Medicine. 2020; cited 2022 July. Available via: https://www.anzca.edu.au/resources/college-publications/acute-pain-management/apmse5.pdf
Safe use of opioids for surgical patients. In: NPS Medicine Wise [online]. 2022; cited 2022 July. Available via: https://www.nps.org.au/safe-use-of-opioids-in-acute-pain
Macintyre P, Schug A. Acute Pain Medicine: a Practical Guide 5e. Boca Raton: CRC Press; 2021.

Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care. Opioid Analgesic Stewardship in Acute Pain Clinical Care Standard -Acute care edition [Internet]. 2022. Available from: https://www.safetyandquality.gov.au/sites/default/files/2022-04/opioid-analgesic-stewardship-in-acute-pain-clinical-care-standard.pdf
